title: The Internet & AI

vocab.artificial intelligence: computer systems that can perform tasks that normally need human intelligence
vocab.algorithm: a set of rules followed by a computer to solve problems
vocab.privacy: the right to keep your personal information secret
vocab.misinformation: false information that is spread, whether or not the person knows it is false
vocab.addicted: unable to stop using or doing something

phrases: On the one hand... on the other hand...

questions: How much time do you spend online every day?
Do you think the internet has made life easier?
Have you ever used an AI tool?
What do you mainly use the internet for?
Do you worry about your online privacy?
answers: I would say I spend about four or five hours online every day. That includes schoolwork, messaging friends, and watching videos.
I think the internet has made many things easier, like communication and research. However, it can also be distracting and sometimes overwhelming.
Yes, I have used AI tools for help with homework and grammar checking. They are useful, but I make sure I understand the answers myself.
I mainly use the internet for school, social media, and entertainment. I also use it to learn new skills through tutorials.
Yes, I do worry about privacy. I try not to share too much personal information, but it is hard to know who can see your data.

part2.question: How might AI change the way students learn in the future?
part2.photoA: A student studying alone with books and handwritten notes
part2.photoB: A student using a tablet with an AI learning assistant
part2.model: In photo A, a student is learning in a traditional way with books and notes, which helps develop independent thinking. In photo B, a student is using AI on a tablet, which can give instant feedback and personalised exercises. I think AI will change learning by making it more flexible, but it could also make students lazy if they rely on it too much. The best approach is probably a balance: students use AI for support but still do their own thinking and writing.

part3.task: A school is deciding how students should use AI responsibly. Discuss the ideas and decide which two rules would be most important.
options: AI can only be used for checking grammar
Students must declare when they use AI
Teachers must explain how AI works
AI can be used for research but not writing essays
part3.model:
A: I think students should have to declare when they use AI. That makes them honest and responsible.
B: That's a good point, but teachers also need to explain how AI works so students understand its limits.
A: I agree. Banning AI completely is unrealistic because students will use it anyway.
B: Yes. Allowing AI for research but not for writing essays seems fair. It helps students learn without doing the work for them.
A: So let's choose declaring AI use and limiting it to research.
B: Yes, I agree with you.

questions: Is social media good for young people?
How can people avoid misinformation online?
What laws should exist for AI?
answers: Social media can be good for connecting with friends, but it can also harm mental health. It depends on how much time you spend and what content you follow.
I do not think AI should replace teachers. It can help with marking and explanations, but teachers understand emotions and motivate students.
People should check information from multiple sources and avoid trusting headlines without reading the full article. Critical thinking is essential.
There should be laws about data privacy, transparent AI decisions, and clear rules about who is responsible when AI causes harm.

homework.reading: Read an article about the benefits and risks of AI. Summarise both sides in four sentences.
homework.listening: Watch a short documentary about the internet. Note three advantages and three disadvantages mentioned.
homework.writing: Write an essay (140–190 words): 'Do the benefits of the internet outweigh the disadvantages?'
